title: Lesson 212
transcript: Most organizations nowadays have a huge number of different service providers that they use for all sorts of different services across the organization. And companies need to be able to understand, are their service providers actually doing what they're supposed to be doing? Are they actually providing the services that they're supposed to be providing? And are those services secure? In this section, we're going to dig a little bit into how companies can gain some level of assurance that their service providers are actually doing what they're supposed to be doing. Now, one way that a company could do this is they could conduct their own audit. So let's talk about some different audit approaches, and this doesn't necessarily specifically relate to service providers. So let's first talk about internal audits. An internal audit is an audit conducted by a company using its own auditors on its own systems. So it's entirely internal to the organization. That's an internal audit. An external audit is where an organization sends their auditors out to audit a service provider. So a good example of this would be if a customer was using Amazon Web Services, AWS, as their cloud provider, they would send their auditors to AWS to audit the AWS environment. That would be known as an external audit. And that's actually a really good example of why we have a third type of audit known as third party audits. So let's reflect for a moment. How many customers does Amazon have? millions. And if Amazon even let a small percentage, a quarter of 1% of their customers in to audit their environment, they would be overrun by auditors. And so what Amazon does in this case is they hire an external auditor, they hire a third party auditor, sorry, they hire a third party auditor to come in and audit their environment for them. This is known as third party audit. And the reason we call it third party audit is because there are three parties involved. There's the customer, the service provider and the independent auditor. And the way this arrangement typically works is that a service provider will hire an independent auditor to come in and audit their environment, produce a report, which can then be handed out to all the service providers' customers. And the reason that this is very popular nowadays for an organization to get assurance is that just imagine you're a company and you go to your service provider and you ask them, how secure are your controls? How secure are the services that you're providing to me? What are they going to say to you? I mean, it would obviously be in their best interest to say, oh yeah, our services are perfect, don't worry at all, best ever. But as a consumer, are you truly gonna believe them? And I suggest you probably shouldn't. And so that's why we use third party audit. And that's why it's so consistently used nowadays. It's not the company, the service provider itself that's auditing their services. It's an independent auditor, a third party that's doing it. Now, the most common type of third-party audit report nowadays is the SOC 2 report. So let's dig into SOC 2 reports and understand what they look like and how they fit together and what they provide to the consumer. Now, before I start explaining SOC 2 reports, I think it's useful to understand who is the organization that created SOC 2 reports. And the reason that I think this is useful is there's actually three types of SOC reports and then two subtypes, and it can all get rather confusing. So by giving you this bit of context, I think it'll help you to remember what these different SOC reports are. So what is the organization behind the SSAE 18 standard? In the US, it's the AICPA. And the AICPA is made up of financial auditors. So what do you think a SOC 1 report focuses on? financial reporting related issues. And to be honest, as a security professional, we don't really care about SOC 1 reports. Now SOC 2 reports, they focus on what are known as the five trust principles. And these five trust principles are known as security, availability, confidentiality, processing integrity, and privacy. Now, as security professionals, these are absolutely the things that we care about. So SOC 2 reports, very useful to security professionals. Now, a SOC 2 report actually contains a fair amount of fairly confidential information. So organizations, service providers don't want to just hand out SOC 2 reports to just anyone. And so that was actually why the SOC 3 standard was created. A SOC 3 report is a sanitized, summarized version of a SOC 2 report. And to be totally honest, as a security professional, we don't really care about SOC 3 reports because they're basically just a marketing tool. They don't tell us any of the information that we're truly interested in knowing to get the assurance that we need that the processes are working properly at our service provider. Now, just to make this slightly more confusing, there's actually two types of SOC 1 and SOC 2 reports. So there's a SOC 1, type 1, type 2, and a SOC 2, type 1, type 2. So what is this type 1, type 2 thing? It's actually fairly simple. here's what a type 1 report covers it covers design at a point in time what does that mean well design means the auditor will come in and look to see if the controls are designed properly it's basically just a paper-based exercise they're looking at the policies the procedures things like that to see if the control is designed at a point in time, so on like the Monday when they come in and conduct their audit. Does that tell you if the control is actually operating effectively? Not in the least. So type one just covers design at a point in time and frankly, not that helpful for us as security professionals. But a type two report covers everything a type one report covers. It covers design, but it also covers operating effectiveness, not just at a point in time, but over a period of time. typically a year and here's what a type 2 audit looks like the auditor in order to test that the control is both designed and operating effectively will typically come in and let's use change management as an example the auditor will come in and first see if the change management process has been designed appropriately So they'll first look to see if there's a proper policy and procedures and standards and so forth, if the control is designed properly. And then they'll test to see if it's operating effectively by looking at the entire population of changes that have occurred in, say, a year. and then taking a sampling of those changes and testing each of those changes to make sure that it followed all the policies, all the procedures, to make sure that the right initiation for the impact assessment for the change was done, that the right approvals were in place, that the right testing was done, that the right back-out plans were created, that the right regression testing was done, and all this was documented. If all that was done properly, then the control is usually found to be operating effectively. So in summary, as security professionals, what do we want? We want SOC 2 reports because they cover the five trust principles, and we want a SOC 2 type 2 because it covers design and operating effectiveness. One final, but very important slide here. And that is the different roles that are typically involved in an audit. This is probably very useful information for you to remember. So here's a quick summary of what are some of the major roles that you'd have involved in your compliance function and what would they do? So at the highest level, you have your senior management. And what are they doing? They're providing the right tone from the top. They're supporting the compliance function, making sure that it's well-funded, that it has the support of senior management, etc. Below them, you're going to have your audit committee. Your audit committee is made up of stakeholders from around the organization that can provide input on the compliance function, making sure it's looking at the right things, is aware of the right regulations, understands the right parts of the business, and so forth. Below that, your security officer is going to advise from a security perspective on what the audit should cover from a security perspective, how the results should be interpreted. You'll also have your compliance manager. Your compliance manager is specifically responsible for making sure the compliance function is created, staffed, conducting audits, running properly. And then you have your internal auditors and your external auditors. Your internal auditors are an organization's own auditors auditing the organization's own processes. And external auditors are auditors that are brought in. So this would be where a company would hire an audit firm to come in and audit their controls. Therefore, those are external auditors. And the reason that an organization would typically bring in external auditors is because they want an independent, unbiased view on things. So those are some of the major roles that you should remember related to the compliance function.
